methods_used: Random Forest MDI (Mean Decrease in Impurity)
metadata.ranking_method: Consensus — average rank position across all 3 methods

tier_1_highest.label: Tier 1 — Highest Importance
tier_1_highest.description: Top predictors that appear in top ranks across all 3 methods. Most robust and reliable features for mental state classification. Should always be included in any model.

features.name: overall_sentiment_score
features.reason: Single most direct signal — negative sentiment strongly correlates with distress across all classes

features.name: fear_word_frequency
features.reason: Strongly linked to Anxiety and PTSD classes; clinical indicator of heightened threat perception

features.name: sadness_word_frequency
features.reason: Strongly linked to Depression; high sadness word usage mirrors clinical low mood symptoms

features.name: negative_emotion_ratio
features.reason: Broad negative emotion signal — captures overall emotional negativity across all distressed mental states

features.name: self_reference_density
features.reason: High I/me/my usage is a well-established clinical marker for depression and self-focused rumination

features.name: rumination_phrase_frequency
features.reason: Repetitive negative thinking patterns in speech are core symptoms of both depression and anxiety

tier_2_strong.label: Tier 2 — Strong Importance
tier_2_strong.description: Reliable secondary predictors that appear in the top 20 of most methods. Add significant value alongside Tier 1 features.

features.name: positive_emotion_ratio
features.reason: Inverse of negative emotion — key for separating the Normal/healthy class from distressed classes

features.name: uncertainty_word_frequency
features.reason: Words like maybe, I don't know, what if are linguistic markers of anxious thought patterns

features.name: anger_word_frequency
features.reason: Elevated anger expression is a distinguishing marker for Bipolar disorder and PTSD classes

features.name: semantic_coherence_score
features.reason: Psychosis and mania show low coherence; depression shows coherent but narrow, negative-focused speech

features.name: future_focus_ratio
features.reason: Depression keeps people stuck in past tense; anxiety fixates on feared future outcomes

features.name: semantic_embeddings (emb_0 to emb_31)
features.reason: 32-dimensional dense semantic embeddings of the speech/text. They encode rich contextual meaning beyond surface-level word counts — capturing tone, topic, and intent. Individual dimensions are not interpretable on their own, but collectively they carry strong discriminative signal confirmed by all 3 importance methods.

tier_3_moderate.label: Tier 3 — Moderate Importance
tier_3_moderate.description: Provide supporting signal but are less consistent across methods. Useful as supplementary features, especially in tree-based models.

features.name: total_word_count
features.reason: Depression typically produces fewer words; manic states produce more — verbosity is a behavioural signal

features.name: ttr
features.reason: Type-Token Ratio measures vocabulary diversity — low TTR can indicate cognitive slowdown or fixation

features.name: filler_word_frequency
features.reason: High filler usage (um, uh, like) indicates cognitive load, disorganised thought, or anxiety

features.name: repetition_rate
features.reason: Rumination manifests as repetition of words and phrases in speech

features.name: topic_shift_frequency
features.reason: Rapid topic shifting is a marker for mania; staying stuck on one negative topic signals depression

features.name: parse_tree_depth
features.reason: Syntactic sentence complexity varies with cognitive state — shallow trees can indicate disorganised thought

tier_4_lower.label: Tier 4 — Lower Importance
tier_4_lower.description: Weak standalone predictors. Either redundant with higher-tier features or too dataset-specific. Consider dropping these to reduce noise.

features.name: past_focus_ratio
features.reason: Some signal but heavily overlaps with future_focus_ratio and sentiment features

features.name: present_focus_ratio
features.reason: Very weak standalone signal — does not reliably distinguish mental states

features.name: noun_ratio
features.reason: Mild POS-level signal, mostly already captured by emotional and embedding features

features.name: verb_ratio
features.reason: Mild POS-level signal, mostly already captured by emotional and embedding features

features.name: adjective_ratio
features.reason: Mild POS-level signal, mostly already captured by emotional and embedding features

features.name: adverb_ratio
features.reason: Mild POS-level signal, mostly already captured by emotional and embedding features

features.name: language_model_perplexity
features.reason: Useful for detecting severe speech disorganisation, but noisy and inconsistent across classes

features.name: unique_word_count
features.reason: Largely redundant — total_word_count and ttr already capture the same information

recommended_model_features.description: Final recommended feature set for the Mindspace classifier — Tier 1 + Tier 2 only. Confirmed predictive by all 3 importance methods.

features.name: overall_sentiment_score
features.reason: Single most direct signal for mental state — negative sentiment strongly correlates with distress across all classes

features.name: fear_word_frequency
features.reason: Strongly linked to Anxiety and PTSD classes; presence of fear words is a clinical indicator of heightened threat perception

features.name: sadness_word_frequency
features.reason: Strongly linked to Depression; high sadness word usage mirrors clinical low mood symptoms

features.name: negative_emotion_ratio
features.reason: Captures overall emotional negativity — a broad signal that spans all distressed mental states

features.name: self_reference_density
features.reason: High I/me/my usage is a well-established clinical marker for depression and self-focused rumination

features.name: rumination_phrase_frequency
features.reason: Repetitive negative thinking patterns expressed in speech are core symptoms of both depression and anxiety

features.name: positive_emotion_ratio
features.reason: Inverse of negative emotion — key for separating the Normal/healthy class from all distressed classes

features.name: uncertainty_word_frequency
features.reason: Words like maybe, I don't know, what if are direct linguistic markers of anxious thought patterns

features.name: anger_word_frequency
features.reason: Elevated anger expression is a distinguishing marker for Bipolar disorder and PTSD classes

features.name: semantic_coherence_score
features.reason: Measures logical connectedness of speech — psychosis and mania show low coherence; depression shows narrow but coherent negative speech

features.name: future_focus_ratio
features.reason: Temporal language focus is diagnostic — depression keeps people in the past; anxiety fixates on feared future outcomes

embedding_features.reason: 32-dimensional dense semantic embeddings of the speech/text. They encode rich contextual meaning that goes beyond surface-level word counts — capturing tone, topic, and intent. Individual dimensions are not independently interpretable, but collectively they carry strong discriminative signal confirmed by all 3 importance methods.

features_to_drop.description: These features are redundant, weak, or already captured by stronger features. Dropping them reduces noise without hurting model performance.

features.name: unique_word_count
features.reason: Redundant with total_word_count and ttr

features.name: past_focus_ratio
features.reason: Overlaps with future_focus_ratio and sentiment features

features.name: present_focus_ratio
features.reason: Weak standalone signal that does not reliably distinguish mental states

features.name: noun_ratio
features.reason: Already captured by emotional and embedding features

features.name: verb_ratio
features.reason: Already captured by emotional and embedding features

features.name: adjective_ratio
features.reason: Already captured by emotional and embedding features

features.name: adverb_ratio
features.reason: Already captured by emotional and embedding features
